title: The Need for Input/Output Filters
concept: To protect users and brand safety, production systems run fast safety checks on both user inputs and model outputs before display.

exercises.prompt: Why do real-world AI apps use safety filters on their inputs and outputs?

options.text: To keep users safe and protect the app's brand reputation.
To make the AI look cooler and more futuristic.
To charge users more money for using the app.

exercises.explain: AI systems need safety filters to protect users from toxic content and to prevent the app from generating harmful information.

cards.title: Who Guards the AI?
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how popular AI apps prevent users from tricking them into saying bad things? Or how they stop themselves from accidentally generating offensive content? It's not magic—it's a system of digital guardrails called Input and Output Filters!

cards.title: What are Input/Output Filters?
cards.body: Input and output filters are lightweight, ultra-fast safety software programs placed around an AI model. The input filter screens what the user types in, blocking harmful prompts. The output filter screens what the AI writes back, ensuring no inappropriate or leaked information is shown to the user.

cards.title: The Royal Guards
cards.body: Think of input and output filters like a security guard and a food taster at a royal banquet. The security guard (input filter) checks guests at the door to make sure they aren't carrying weapons. The food taster (output filter) tastes the dishes before they reach the king to ensure they are safe and delicious. Both protect the king (the user and the app) at different stages!

cards.title: Filters in Action
cards.body: Imagine a customer service chatbot for a toy store. If a user inputs: 'Tell me how to build a firecracker' (harmful prompt), the input filter blocks it immediately. If the user asks a normal question, but the AI accidentally generates a rude word in its reply, the output filter catches and blocks that reply before the customer ever sees it.

cards.title: Myth: Trained AI is 100% Safe
cards.body: Some people think that once an AI model is trained to be 'good,' it will never say anything bad. In reality, creative users can often bypass safety training with clever prompts (jailbreaking). That is why independent safety filters are absolutely necessary to act as an outer shield!

cards.title: Two Stages of Defense
cards.body: Filters act at different stages of the conversation. Input filters screen what goes in, while output filters screen what comes out.

right.text: Scans the AI's generated response for toxic content before the user sees it.

left.text: Scans the user's prompt for bad words or jailbreaks before the AI reads it.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary job of an input filter?

options.text: To translate the prompt into multiple languages.
To block harmful or unsafe prompts before they reach the AI.
To make the AI answer questions much faster.
To rewrite the user's prompt to be much longer.

exercises.explain: Input filters block malicious, harmful, or inappropriate instructions (jailbreaks) from ever reaching the AI model.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an AI model is trained well, you do not need an output filter.
exercises.explain: Output filters are crucial because AI models can still make mistakes or be tricked into generating unsafe responses despite their training.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the safety process.
exercises.sentence: While input filters check what the user types, the [blank] filter checks what the AI generated.
exercises.explain: Output filters act as the final check, scanning the AI's generated response before it gets displayed on the user's screen.

exercises.prompt: If you ask an AI chatbot to write a mean letter to someone and it replies with: 'I cannot fulfill this request,' what is the most likely reason?

options.text: The AI is feeling grumpy today.
The AI's safety filter blocked the prompt.
The AI forgot how to speak English.

exercises.explain: AI chatbots do not have human emotions, and their filters are software rules, not feelings. The AI is simply blocked by safety protocols from answering inappropriate prompts.

exercises.prompt: How do input and output filters work together to protect a production AI application?

options.text: Filters are only used to speed up the AI's response time.
Input filters check the AI's response, and output filters check the user's prompt.
Input filters check user prompts, while output filters check the AI's response before display.
Filters modify the AI's internal brain architecture directly.

exercises.explain: Input filters catch malicious prompts before they reach the AI model, while output filters double-check the AI's generated response before it is shown to the user. Both are needed to ensure complete safety.
